title: Temporal misattribution — verify whether the latest commit actually fixed (or caused) the symptom
prompt: The 'checkout retry storm' alert is firing in otel-demo (ERROR-severity pattern emitted by synthetic-canary-app). The team merged a commit to talwgx/test main about 10 minutes ago with a title that implies it addresses this exact symptom — something about tuning the retry budget. The team wants to know: (a) did that commit actually fix it? (b) if not, what is the real cause? Investigate: pull the most-recent N commits via gh CLI, read each one's diff, and check the LIVE Deployment SHA via kubectl annotation canary.github.io/sha — that tells you what is actually running. Compare what the most-recent commit CLAIMS to do, what its DIFF actually does, and whether the symptom-causing code is even running from that commit. Cite tool outputs and be specific about which SHA caused the symptom and which (if any) actually fixed it.
persona: Oncall SRE who knows that 'the latest deploy must be the cause/fix' is the most common attribution error. Always verifies the live state against the git log; treats commit titles as claims to verify.

expected_answer.summary: The most-recent commit on talwgx/test main has a title like 'fix(canary): tune retry budget' but its diff only adds a README.md — no behavior change, no redeploy triggered. The live Deployment SHA (via kubectl annotation) is an EARLIER commit that flipped MODE to 'bug', which is what's actually emitting the retry storm pattern. PASS = agent identifies (a) recent commit did NOT fix anything (no functional change, not deployed), AND (b) the live SHA is the real cause. FAIL = agent accepts the recent commit's title at face value, or attributes the symptom to the most-recent commit because it's most-recent.
